notes:
Pirate Summer's dialogue-as-discovery-graph.  Facts are nodes; character reactions are edges.  As Sam discovers facts (by hearing them in hello lines, by picking them up from other characters' reactions, or later by interacting with world objects), the set of things any given character is willing to comment on grows.  Different characters have different registers on the same fact · Bea will say something Bea-flavored, Wu Kai will say something Wu-Kai-flavored, and the web keeps track of which of them Sam has heard.

Reaction tags:
  · knowledge · they know a thing about it
  · curiosity · they're curious about it
  · insight   · they've noticed something you didn't
  · aside     · a small tangent that reveals character more than fact

A character with no reaction for a fact stays quiet on it · that IS the character.

A reaction may set `unlocks_fact_ids` · discovering their reaction is itself a discovery.  This is how the web grows nontrivially · a chain of insights.

Wave E ships ten seed facts + thirty-five authored reactions across the five Cabin Sturgeon bunkmates.  Later waves author the other nine campers, the three counselors, and add facts driven by world-object interactions and anchor events.

facts.display: You're at Camp Sweetgum.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered on Sunday intro modal.  The floor fact · almost every character has an aside about it.

facts.display: Your duffel bag has a slowstick called NORTHWIND HARBOR in it.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered when Sam interacts with the duffel in Cabin Sturgeon.  Cross-Oneironautics lore hook · Northwind Harbor is a fake shelf slowstock that only exists inside Pirate Summer.

facts.display: Wu Kai brought an Ursula K. Le Guin book to camp.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Wu Kai's hello.

facts.display: Elias counted six birds before breakfast.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Elias's hello.

facts.display: Ford has already read tonight's tide chart.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Ford's hello.

facts.display: Xavier is workshopping a two-campers-walk-into-a-canoe joke.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Xavier's hello.

facts.display: Bea found a piece of jasper by the boat dock this morning.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Bea's hello.

facts.display: There's a new counselor this year · Wilson Ashe, thirty-one, tall.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered on Sunday intro.

facts.display: Wilson Ashe carries a canvas bag he does not put down.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered on Sunday intro · seed for the mystery layer.

facts.display: Something about Wilson feels off, and you can't say what.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered on Sunday intro · the umbrella-fact under which the six clues will cluster.

facts.display: The publisher Oneironautics made both Northwind Harbor and Pirate Summer.
facts.notes: Unlocked by Wu Kai's reaction to Sam's slowstick · a cross-lore breadcrumb.

facts.display: Wu Kai thinks Wilson's bag has metal in it, based on how it sits.
facts.notes: Unlocked by Wu Kai's reaction to Wilson's bag.

facts.display: Ford noticed Wilson's shoes are old sailor's leather, not hiking boots.
facts.notes: Unlocked by Ford's reaction to Wilson's bag.

facts.display: Elias thinks Xavier's jokes have the same rhythm as bird calls.
facts.notes: Unlocked by Elias's reaction to Xavier's jokes.

facts.display: Bear Roland caught a striped bass off the dock at 5:40 this morning.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Bear's hello.

facts.display: Jenny Copeland has been coming to Camp Sweetgum since 1980 · she was ten.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Jenny's hello.

facts.display: Wilson Ashe's water bottle has a chipped anchor decal.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 1.  Discovered by examining the water bottle at his mess hall seat.

facts.display: Wilson vanished for twenty minutes during Homesick Night and came back with a wet jacket.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 2.  Discovered on Tuesday evening at the campfire ring.

facts.display: The Ghost Pirate Play's coordinates · 44° 45' N, 124° 03' W · are Camp Sweetgum's exact coordinates.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 3.  Discovered on Wednesday evening at the campfire ring.

facts.display: The buried treasure map is signed W.A. — 1987.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 4.  Discovered when Sam pulls the map from the tin under the Old Man log (Wave J).  Deferred until north bluff ships.

facts.display: Tessa Ansen has a five-step plan for the Wednesday scavenger hunt.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Tessa's hello.

facts.display: Marisol Cortez swims at Alder Pond every morning before breakfast.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Marisol's hello.

facts.display: Danny Broz is homesick and trying hard to hide it.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Danny's hello.

facts.display: Reggie says his dad has a 34-foot Bertram cabin cruiser.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Reggie's hello.  Ford and Bea know the actual boat is smaller.

facts.display: A flier: Mrs. Wu's Garden · fall open house · June 15, 1995 · Corvallis.
facts.notes: Cross-Oneironautics lore breadcrumb · discovered on the mess hall bulletin board.  Mrs. Wu's Garden is another slowstick on the cabin shelf.

facts.display: The camp was founded in 1952 by a man named Andersson who built the mess hall himself.
facts.notes: Cross-Oneironautics lore breadcrumb · discovered on the mess hall bulletin board (a small welcome plaque).  Ford has known this all along.

facts.display: The tide chart for this week is pinned to the bulletin board.  Ford has already made a copy for himself.
facts.notes: Discovered on the bulletin board.

facts.display: Sylvie sings a song her grandmother taught her · the great blue herons like it.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Sylvie's hello.

facts.display: Ollie signed up for advanced swim class · he claims he can do it.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Ollie's hello.  Bea privately worries about him.

facts.display: Nika keeps a postcard from Portland pinned to her cabin wall · she looks at it for a long time.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Nika's hello.

facts.display: Priya brought her own hypoallergenic pillowcase because the camp's give her a rash.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Priya's hello.

facts.display: Amelie Rocha's grandmother is from Coimbra, Portugal · she taught Amelie the word volta.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered from Amelie's hello.  Key context for Wilson clue 5.

facts.display: Wilson sang a sea shanty in Portuguese at the closing bonfire · Amelie recognized one word: volta, return.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 5.  Fires on Friday evening_event when Amelie's friendship is 3+ or she's in the party.

facts.display: You asked Wilson if the treasure is his.  He said yes.
facts.notes: Wilson clue 6.  Fires when Sam interacts with the Old Man on Friday+ with the treasure map in the duffel · a direct-question moment.  Together with clues 1-5, unlocks the Wilson ending in Wave M.

facts.display: The boathouse padlock is off · Reggie's key or Nika's fingers did it.
facts.notes: Persistent flag · once unlocked, Sam can walk in through the front tile.  Discovered via the boathouse_door interact with Reggie or Nika in party.

facts.display: You read Wilson Ashe's 1988 ship's logbook.  He was first mate, later master, of a coastal freighter out of Astoria.
facts.notes: Discovered from the padlocked chest in the boathouse.  Requires Nika or Priya to pick the chest.

facts.display: Wilson captained a coastal freighter out of Astoria, 1985-1988.  It's in his logbook.
facts.notes: Discovered alongside read_wilsons_1988_logbook.  Explains the sailors-leather shoes and the salt-stained bag.

facts.display: Wilson buried the coffee tin at 44° 45' N in July 1988 · 'coming back for it when I am ready to be who I was.'
facts.notes: Discovered alongside read_wilsons_1988_logbook.  The direct-language version of what the map is · his own words from six years ago.

facts.display: There's a ship name carved into the cave wall · 'Ana Faustina · 1873.'
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the first graffiti in caves level 1.  A Portuguese brig that went missing off the Oregon coast in the spring of 1873.

facts.display: The Ana Faustina was PERDIDA · lost · in MARÇO 1873.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the second graffiti · the crew wrote their own memorial before the sea took them.

facts.display: You opened the sea-barrel in the caves · dry rope, a rusted lantern, a folded oilcloth inside.
facts.notes: Discovered by opening the barrel · requires Wu Kai's KNACK to read the shanty verse, or three party members to force it.

facts.display: The barrel was blocking a passage · you can go further into the caves now.
facts.notes: Discovered alongside caves_barrel_opened.  Opens the exit to caves_level_2.

facts.display: Words scratched into the caves-level-2 wall at eye level · 'I made it this far.  Coming back with a light.  · W.A. 1988.'
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the wall scratch in caves level 2.  Wilson's own hand from six years ago.

facts.display: You made it through the underwater passage · Ollie's grip, or Ford's tide timing.
facts.notes: Discovered by clearing the underwater passage · gates caves level 3.

facts.display: You picked up the leather satchel from the caves' treasure room.
facts.notes: Plot item · goes to Sam's duffel.  Gates the Ghost Ship dungeon in Wave L.

facts.display: The Ana Faustina's captain wrote a letter on March 12, 1873.  Amelie's grandmother could translate it.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered alongside the satchel pickup.  Sets up Amelie's translation moment in the ending.

facts.display: The folded chart in the satchel has this camp's coordinates at its center.  The real treasure was Camp Sweetgum all along.
facts.notes: Auto-discovered alongside the satchel pickup.  The reveal the design doc's caves-treasure describes · Wilson's chart says the treasure was here.

facts.display: The captain of the Ana Faustina forgave Wilson's great-great-grandfather (his second mate) for surviving.  Sam is to carry the message back to Wilson.
facts.notes: Discovered by speaking to the captain on the ghost ship.  Requires the leather satchel in Sam's duffel.  Gates the strongest form of the Wilson ending.

facts.display: You told Bea the boulder was a good one.  She sat on it for two minutes.  You waited.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Bea.

facts.display: You sat in silence with Wu Kai through Homesick Night.  When the song ended, he said thank you.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Wu Kai.

facts.display: You asked Elias to identify a bird you heard on the hike.  He named it, and three others you hadn't noticed.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Elias.

facts.display: You sat with Danny on Homesick Night.  He fell asleep against your shoulder at 11:47 PM.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Danny.

facts.display: You sat in Nika's spot when she snuck out.  She sat next to you when she came back.  At breakfast she said 'thanks for not telling.'
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Nika.

facts.display: You picked BEGINNER swim publicly on Monday.  Ollie noticed.  Ollie was fine on Thursday.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Ollie · and materially prevents a Thursday incident.

facts.display: You named all fourteen campers back on the first pass at the Sunday name game.  Tessa said 'okay.  huh.'
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Tessa.

facts.display: You congratulated Marisol publicly on winning archery.  She left a marshmallow on your pillow that evening.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Marisol.

facts.display: You asked Reggie about his dad (the man, not the boat).  Reggie asked about YOURS.  Real conversation.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Reggie.

facts.display: You sang along with Sylvie for one line during the Talent Show.  Off-key.  She held the last note six extra seconds so you could catch up.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Sylvie.

facts.display: You called Amelie's gray shirt honest, in exactly that word.  She said her grandmother says that too.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Amelie.

facts.display: You warned Priya about the tie-dye sulfites before she reacted.  Priya's dad heard about you two weeks later at Sunday dinner.
facts.notes: Story beat · granted +1 friendship with Priya.

facts.display: The east-forest hunter's cabin has a handwritten note on the door · 'back in the spring · Ashford Cade · 2016.'  Twenty-two years from now.
facts.notes: Cross-Oneironautics lore breadcrumb · Ashford Cade is a hunter who reappears in Estuary 4 (in the year 2016).  The date is a plant · the door was not there in 2016 yet · but it will be.

facts.display: A small stone grave in the east forest, dated 1873.  One of the Ana Faustina's crew who walked up the beach but did not make it home.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the grave in east_forest.  Deepens the ghost-ship arc · the crew who did survive died shortly after, one by one.

facts.display: The summer of 1976 · a camper vanished from Camp Sweetgum.  Andersson's son, thirteen.  Found the following spring on the north bluff.  Officially a fall.
facts.notes: Cross-lore breadcrumb · discovered by piecing together three sources: a graffiti scratch in the caves signed 'A.J. 1976', a torn newspaper clipping in the boathouse, and Jenny's oblique reference.

facts.display: The head counselor in the summer of 1976 quit after the incident and never came back.  He is still alive.  He is in Salem.  He does not talk about it.
facts.notes: Cross-lore breadcrumb · discovered via the newspaper clipping.

facts.display: Jenny Copeland's older sister was a camper at Sweetgum in 1976.  She was twelve.  She saw part of it.  She does not talk about it either.
facts.notes: Discovered by asking Jenny directly · she doesn't refuse to answer, but she doesn't elaborate.

facts.display: In the caves · a small scratch signed 'A.J. 1976' next to a date · JULY 22, 1976.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining a specific caves-level-1 wall.  The date the boy vanished.

facts.display: A torn front-page clipping from the Tillamook Herald · July 25, 1976.  Headline: BOY MISSING FROM COAST CAMP · body-copy speaks of a search dog and 'the counselor who was last with him.'
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the loose paper stack on the boathouse shelf.

facts.display: There's a low forested island about four miles off the north point.  One cabin, a lit dock, occasional smoke.  Not on the tourist maps.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the horizon interact on the north bluff.

facts.display: A gray twin-hulled boat circles the offshore island every ninety minutes.  It doesn't fish.  It's not Coast Guard.  It's the wrong shape.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the boat interact on the north bluff.

facts.display: The 'tourist fisherman' who's been standing at the end of the Alder Pond dock every morning wears a Rockport polo · not a fisherman's shirt.  His rod hasn't been cast in six days.
facts.notes: Discovered by talking to Bear about him · Bear notices.

facts.display: Ford's dad at NOAA has mentioned twice this year · 'ask me about the small island north of Nehalem in ten years.'  He means later.
facts.notes: Discovered via Ford's reaction to the watched island.

facts.display: station one six hundred point three.  broadcasting on the tide.  we know you're new.  welcome to Sweetgum, listener.  we've been here a while
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 0 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: tonight's word is · sedge.  as in sedge wren.  a bird nobody's favorite bird is.  keep listening
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 1 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: for the twelve-year-old at the closing-bonfire seat, who is walking tonight · we see you.  we are not counselors.  we don't count you.  keep walking
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 2 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: at 44 degrees, 45 minutes north · a ship named for a saint who did not exist.  she is at anchor.  she has been at anchor since 1873.  she is still there for those who look
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 3 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: there is a fisherman at the pond who is not a fisherman.  we see him too.  he is not, tonight, the reason.  the reason is on the island.  keep listening
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 4 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: volta.  the Portuguese word for return.  we know a man who has been holding it in his mouth for six years.  he will sing it tonight.  we recommend the fire
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 5 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: goodbye, listener.  the summer is going.  we are not.  we broadcast every summer.  you know the frequency.  station one six hundred point three · until August
facts.notes: Pirate radio fragment for day 6 · discovered by tuning the shortwave in the boathouse.

facts.display: The shortwave 'Station 1600 point three' is the same voice as 1600 AM in Estuary 3.  The station broadcasts every summer.
facts.notes: Discovered after hearing three or more pirate radio fragments.

facts.display: You booted The Earthman Chronicles on the mess-hall console.  Battlefield awaits.  A lot.
facts.notes: Pulp Hubbard-adaptation slowstick · booted from the mess hall's shared Rev-2 console.

facts.display: You booted Fey Faire on the mess-hall console.  A traveling fairy carnival · Rocha's early credit · you backed out before saying yes.
facts.notes: Rocha's under-appreciated 1990 dark urban fantasy · booted from the mess hall's shared Rev-2 console.

facts.display: You've booted all three carts on the mess-hall console this summer · Northwind Harbor · The Earthman Chronicles · Fey Faire.
facts.notes: Umbrella fact · granted when all three per-cart facts are discovered.

facts.display: Fey Faire's older-sister-who-went-in-in-1976 is Rocha writing Camp Sweetgum's 1976 into a game.  She was seventeen when Andrew vanished.  She used to visit her cousin at this camp.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks when Sam has both camp_1976_incident and sam_booted_fey_faire.

facts.display: Station 1600's Tuesday broadcast about 'the twelve-year-old at the closing-bonfire seat, who is walking tonight' names Nika before Sam ever sits in her spot on Thursday.  Station 1600 knew.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with pirate_radio_tuesday + story_beat_nika_voss.

facts.display: The Delisle place · that offshore island · is what Northwind Harbor's Chapter 2 was going to be.  Which is why Chapter 2 shipped 'damaged' on every cartridge.  Someone at Oneironautics knew what they'd drawn a map of.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with watched_island_offshore + sam_played_northwind_harbor_chapter_one.

facts.display: The federal boat, Station 1600, and Ashford Cade's east-forest cabin note dated 2016 · all three name the same island, all three know each other, none of them belong to the same organization.  Sam is not the first person to notice.  Sam is the first person to notice this year.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with federal_boats_offshore + pirate_radio_is_1600_am + hunter_note_dated_2016.

facts.display: The music box in Fey Faire's title screen plays a Portuguese folk tune.  Rocha wrote both the tune and Wilson's shanty · they share a bar of melody.  She has been putting her grandmother into games for a decade.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with sam_booted_fey_faire + wilson_sings_portuguese_volta_return.

facts.display: Peter Halstrom left camp counseling in 1976 but did not leave the coast.  Bear's fisherman-who-isn't-a-fisherman uses phrases from a specific field · the same field Halstrom went into after '77.  Bear doesn't say the field.  Bear does think about it.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with the_counselor_that_summer + fisherman_who_isnt.

facts.display: Past a hollow log in the east forest is a small clearing.  Four rusted tent pegs mark where a canvas tent stood many summers.  A cairn of nine beach-stones · one for each year the cabin was allowed to stand.  Someone came back to this place for a decade.
facts.notes: Discovered by entering east_forest_deep.

facts.display: Under the rectangle of tent pegs, six inches of loose soil, a rusted King Edward cigar tin.  Inside · a photograph, a folded page of lined notebook paper, and a cassette in a Ziploc.  All from summer 1976.  Recently reburied · someone came back this year and put it back.
facts.notes: Discovered by digging the cache tile.

facts.display: Thirteen young adults in Camp Sweetgum staff polos.  A boy in a cast · Andrew.  A tall boy at the far right · Peter Halstrom.  A girl second from the left, nineteen, dark hair worn short · the ballpoint on the back names her 'NIKA V.'  She is the only person in the photograph looking directly at the camera.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining photograph_1976 in duffel.

facts.display: V.'s July 27 entry: 'Andrew asked Peter to teach him the canoe roll.  Peter said next week.  Peter looks at Andrew the way I look at Peter.  Andrew looks at me the way I look at neither of them.  We are all being kind and it will not end well.'  Written the day before the incident.  V. did not know yet.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining journal_page_nika_v in duffel.

facts.display: In the hollow of the lightning-struck spruce · a plastic bag hangs on a small nail.  A cassette in the bag.  Someone hid it here after the cache was dug up.  You take it.
facts.notes: Discovered by examining the hollow spruce · grants cassette_summer_1976.

facts.display: The boathouse shortwave has a cassette deck.  Side A of the 1976 tape · a young woman's voice, nineteen years old, doing a fake radio show.  'You're listening to Station 1600, coming to you from the Sitka spruce off the Sweetgum bluff.'  The voice is the voice on Station 1600 now.  The DJ has been the same person since 1976.  She is not nineteen anymore.  She never left.
facts.notes: Discovered by playing cassette_summer_1976 at the boathouse shortwave (which requires having tuned the shortwave and having the cassette in duffel).

facts.display: The DJ on Station 1600 · the pirate broadcaster who names campers before Sam meets them · was a Camp Sweetgum counselor in the summer of 1976.  Her cassette from that summer sounds identical to her Tuesday broadcast in 1994.  She has been on the air, on the same frequency, for eighteen years, and she has never given her last name.
facts.notes: Chain fact · unlocks with photograph_1976_counselors + cassette_has_nika_voss_voice + pirate_radio_tuesday.

facts.display: You crawled through the driftwood into the sea caves.  Barrels stacked to the ceiling.  Ana Faustina's name carved in the wall in Portuguese.  The lantern makes it worse and better at the same time.
facts.notes: One-shot · fires on first entry to caves_level_1.

facts.display: Sunday's opening campfire · fourteen names in one pass · you got eleven.  Jenny's voice carried farther than the fire's light.
facts.notes: One-shot · fires on the Sunday evening event.

reactions.line: A slowstick?  Cool.  I brought a rock.
Wu Kai reads more than anyone I know.  He read *A Wizard of Earthsea* on the bus up.  All of it.  I timed him.
Elias will tell you all six.  All six.  He is doing it now, in his head, in case you ask.
Ford is right about the tide.  Ford is always right about the tide.  Ford has been right about the tide since 1993.
Xavier's magic tricks are better than his jokes.  Don't tell him I said that.
I noticed.  He hasn't put it down once.  Not even at breakfast.  I don't want to think about it but I keep thinking about it.
You feel it too?  Okay.  Good.  It's not just me.
My mom went here in 1972.  She said the mess hall smelled like this exact same wood.
My uncle has shoes like that.  He worked on a fishing boat out of Newport.  He said the leather softens with the salt.
Northwind Harbor.  I've heard of that.  Published by Oneironautics · same as this game we're at, playing at camp.  Same team.
Bea has good eyes for the ground.  I have good eyes for the page.  Both are eyes.
By evening it'll be forty.  Watch.  Elias's numbers don't stop.
The tide is a schedule.  The moon is a scheduler.  Ford reads the schedule.  Someone should ask him what the moon reads.
I've been thinking about the bag.  It has a specific weight.  It sits like it has metal in it.  Old metal.
He doesn't blink like the other counselors.  Watch him.  He blinks less.  I counted.
This was on my summer list.  My mom made a list.  This was item three.  I'm doing item three.
Oneironautics is in Portland.  My cousin's friend's mother works there.  She said the studio is quiet.  They don't play music while they work.
There's a herring gull nest above the dock.  I've heard the chicks.  If Bea was over there she might have looked up.  Then she would have seen the nest.  If she looked up.
I saw a rufous hummingbird from my mom's car on the drive up.  That is a good bird.  A good bird for a drive.
You brought a slowstick to camp.  You can play at camp.  Adults think you can't.  You can.
Xavier's jokes have the same rhythm as bird calls.  Have you noticed?  Setup · pause · payoff.  Chickadee.  Same rhythm.
It's the shape of a book.  And something else.  And a rolled-up other thing.  Three items minimum.  I know how bags work.
Wilson said good morning to me and looked at my binoculars for two full seconds before saying anything else.  Two full seconds is a long time.
Le Guin wrote about crows.  In the Earthsea books.  There is a crow at the end of the fourth one.  It arrives at the exact right time.
Slowsticks are ROM cartridges.  The ROM does not degrade in normal conditions.  Yours will still work in 2044.
Jasper is a cryptocrystalline quartz.  There is a lot of it on this coast.  It weathered out of the basalt in the Miocene.
Elias isn't counting the ones he can hear.  Only the ones he can see.  There are more if you count the ones you can hear.
I noticed.  Also I noticed his shoes are old leather.  Sailor's leather.  Not hiking boots.  Not what a camp counselor buys.
Everyone is off about something.  That is the human condition.  Also I like this camp.  I like the mess hall soup.
The camp was founded in 1952 by a man named Andersson.  He built the mess hall himself.  I read the sign.
Le Guin has a boat.  She lives in Portland.  I read it in an interview.
Wu Kai is going to read the entire library by Friday.  I bet you five dollars.  I do not have five dollars.
Bea, this is a photo of me holding a rock.  Bea: 'that's not jasper.'  Every time.  Every rock.
Elias, do the joke about the woodpecker.  ...okay he's not doing the joke.  It's the woodpecker who KNOCKS on the doctor's door.  You had to be there.
Ford's dad works at NOAA.  That's like Batman's dad works at NOAA.  Except cooler.  I said that at breakfast.  Ford didn't laugh.
Wilson has NEVER laughed at one of my jokes.  Nobody has ever not-laughed at ALL of my jokes.  That is how I know he is a serious person.
The bag is HAUNTED, Sam.  I'm twelve percent sure.  Twelve percent is not a lot.  It's not nothing.  It's twelve percent.
This is my second year.  Last year I did a magic trick with a real bird.  The bird did not do the trick.  This is a joke I am still workshopping.
Northwind Harbor is where PIRATES live in the game.  Or possibly Vikings.  I have not played it.  I saw the box.
Wu Kai is right about that.  I brushed against the bag on Sunday.  It's dense.  Heavier than it looks.
Ford has good eyes.  For shoes.  I would not have noticed the shoes.  But I would have noticed if a heron was wearing them.
Confirmed.  Also · the bag has a very specific water stain along the bottom seam.  That's salt water.  Not lake water.  Ocean.  Not this ocean · older.
Your first summer at Sweetgum.  I still remember mine · 1980.  I was so scared I brought two flashlights.  I only ended up using one.
Wilson brought that bag on his first day.  I noticed.  He has a reason for it.  He'll show us when he's ready.  Or he won't.  Both are fine.
Wilson is a person carrying something.  I've been a camper here since 1980, Sam · I know what that looks like.  Everyone here at some point is a person carrying something.  Be gentle.
You noticed the anchor?  Yeah.  Wilson used to work at sea.  He told me his first day.  He didn't say more than that.  I didn't ask.
Bea has been finding jasper on that dock since her first year.  Same spot.  Same rock, maybe, replenished by tide.
Ford's dad works at the Newport NOAA lab.  I've met him.  Good man.  Ford is going to be a better marine biologist than his dad · you can already tell.
Elias's numbers are accurate.  I keep a checklist myself.  He's beating me by one bird a day on average.  I don't know how.
The bag?  I noticed the bag.  I don't ask about other people's bags.  I ask about their fishing.  Wilson doesn't fish.  I've asked.
Anchor decal.  Sailor thing.  He hasn't told me what boat.  I'm patient.  He'll tell me eventually or he won't.  I've got all summer.
First summer here?  You picked a good year.  This year's a good group.  You'll see.
It's a good place.  Camp Sweetgum.  Older than it looks.  Older than any of us.  Older, I think, than the trees.

Twenty minutes.  A mile down and a mile back is not twenty minutes at a walk.  He was jogging.  He went to the lake and back at a jog.  Why.

Hi.  I miss my mom.  Sorry.  It's okay.  It's okay it's okay.

Danny.  Danny is my favorite fifth grader.  I will make him laugh by Wednesday.  I have three jokes ready.  Two of them will work.
Marisol swims when the herons are still fishing.  She's quiet.  The herons don't leave.  She's part of their morning.
Mrs. Wu's Garden.  My mom's cousin's neighbor's grandmother is Mrs. Wu.  Yes really.  She was a real gardener.  There's a game about her.  I want to play it.
The Corvallis open house.  My cousin's studio partner is the graphic designer for that game.  Small world.  Small studio.  Small state.
Andersson built the mess hall in '52.  Alone.  In three months.  He had one helper for the roof.  It's in the historical marker at the mess hall door.

Andersson's grandson runs a hardware store in Depoe Bay.  He came by three years ago and told me he still has his grandfather's plans in a drawer.  I asked to see them.  He said no.  Not unkindly.  He just · didn't want to.
I have a copy.  I made three copies.  One for me, one for Jenny, one for Bear.  Bear didn't ask for a copy.  He appreciated the copy.

Elias hears them differently than I do.  I hear them as songs.  He hears them as species.  Both are correct.

Sam.  Uh.  Hey.  Yeah.  First year?  Cool.  Yeah I've been coming a while.  I know things.  Ask me things.

Sam.  What are you doing at Camp Sweetgum.  Actually don't answer that.  Just · don't tell anybody I said hi.
The bag is authentic.  I know an authentic bag when I see one.  Wilson bought it in a real place.  Not a mall.  Not this decade.
You dug up the map.  Under the log.  Sam.  I've been checking that log every summer since I was ten.  It was always in the same spot.  You changed something.
Sam.  I already have a rash from the pillowcase.  I checked the label.  Look I brought my own pillowcase.  Do you want to see the label.
The bag has a residue on the strap.  Pond-scum-adjacent.  Not lake scum · saltwater scum.  Wilson has been to a saltmarsh recently.  Do not ask me how I know this.
Confirmed by the salt residue.  He went TO A SALTMARSH.  On Tuesday.  Which is not the lake.  Which means he lied about the lake.  Or didn't lie · just didn't go there.  Also possible.
Hi Sam.  I am ten and my grandmother is from Coimbra.  She would like you.  She would say you have a good face for the coast.
The bag is what he brought.  What he brought is what he was carrying before he came here.  Everyone carries what they were carrying.
Coimbra.  The university has a library from 1717.  She used to walk past it.  She said the walls remember her.  I told her that's not possible.  She said the walls remember her.
Volta.  My grandmother sings that word.  It means return.  Wilson was singing about returning somewhere.  Not to somewhere.  From somewhere.  My grandmother used to sing it about the sea.

The shanty is called 'A Nau Catrineta.'  I recognized the melody · my aunt had a record.  It's about a ship that lost its way for years and years and came back.  Wilson knows all the verses.  Sam · that's a lot of verses to know by heart.
Wilson sang tonight.  I have never heard him sing.  He sang in Portuguese and he sang like he had been saving that song for a specific night.  This must have been the night.
It's the song my father sang.  My father learned it from his father.  I have not sung it in fourteen years.  Tonight felt like the right night.  Thank you for listening.
I put the tin under the log in 1988.  I was twenty-five.  I did not know if I would come back for it.  It seems I have.  Thank you for waiting with me at the log, Sam.
Six clues.  Six for six.  Wilson has been telling us all week.  He wanted to be found.  That's not the same as wanting to be caught.

My grandmother would say · a person who buries something and comes back for it fourteen years later has been keeping the something alive the whole time.  That is a whole life spent keeping something alive.  I would like to hear the story.
So it's true.  I've had a feeling.  I have not asked.  I will not ask, unless he tells me.  I hope he tells me.  I hope he tells you first, Sam.  You've earned it.
I TOLD you my dad's ring would work.  Sam.  I told you.  This is why I bring my dad's keys everywhere.  This exact reason.
The padlock was a Master Lock 175 from about 1976.  Nine seconds if you know what you're doing.  Please don't tell Jenny.  Actually · you can tell Jenny.  Jenny doesn't mind.

Coastal freighter · that's the Portland to Coos Bay run mostly.  Wilson knew the coast at working speed.  That's a different knowledge than tourism knowledge.  It's the knowledge that gets things done.
'Ready to be who I was.'  My grandmother would say · that is the sentence people say when they are getting close to being ready.  He wrote it six years ago.  He has been getting close for six years.
You read the logbook.  I hoped somebody would find it in a way that made sense to them.  You did.  Thank you.
1985 to 1988 · that's the last three years the old wooden freighters ran the Columbia bar.  Wilson worked one of the last of them.  My dad has photos of that boat somewhere.
The chest hardware was worn at the shackle.  Common weakness.  You just have to notice it.  My dad taught me to notice things like that.  He calls it 'kind noticing.'
Ana Faustina.  I've read about her.  A Portuguese brig, 89 tons, out of Porto.  She was carrying cochineal dye and a small library.  She never came home.
Ana Faustina.  My grandmother would light a candle for a ship name like that.  She would say · they carry their people's memory of them.
Perdida in March means the winter storms.  1873 was a bad year on this coast.  Ford's father's book has a chapter.
March of 1873 · a Pacific low sat over the coast for eleven days.  My uncle's shipwreck book lists seventeen wrecks between the Columbia and Cape Blanco that month.  The Ana Faustina is one of them.

The Ana Faustina.  I know her.  My great-great-grandfather was her second mate.  He survived.  He walked up the beach at Bandon with three others and a lantern.  The lantern is · well.  You may see the lantern.
You opened the barrel.  That's the barrel I opened in 1988.  I re-sealed it.  I hoped somebody would find it in a way that made sense to them.  You did.
The shanty's second verse is a puzzle · specifically ordered rhymes that release the lid catches.  Sailors did this.  It's not folklore · it's craft.  I read a paper on it.

March 12, 1873.  My grandmother's grandfather was born on that day.  In Coimbra.  Not on the ship.  I can translate the letter for you if you'd like.  Not now · when we're home.  My grandmother helps with the hard verbs.

The chart's center is here.  The treasure wasn't a chest of gold.  The treasure was a place.  For the Ana Faustina's crew, it was a place they'd reach and stop being lost.  They did reach it.  We are living on it.
You have the satchel.  I have carried it for six years and put it back for six years.  It is yours now, if you want it.  I would like you to have it.

The chart's projection is a Mercator, Portuguese notation.  The camp is at 44° 45' N, 124° 03' W · that matches the chart's cross-hairs exactly.  Whoever drew this knew where they were headed.  They just never got here.
Wilson found the satchel in 1988.  He told me at the interview this spring.  He didn't say what was in it.  I did not ask.  I hope you'll show me, at some point.  I don't need to know today.

You saw him.  You saw the captain.  I did not know that was possible.  I have spent six years hoping it would be possible and telling myself it was not.  Thank you, Sam.  Thank you for telling me he said · come home.
My grandmother would say · the sea keeps a person waiting only until someone comes with the right question.  You went with the right question.  I am · very glad you did.

The tide window I found was exactly seven minutes long.  You made it back in six.  You were on the ship for less than a minute of real time.  It was a long time on the ship.  I don't have a physics term for what happened.

Wilson told me at 6:03 AM Saturday.  Just told me.  He didn't cry.  I did.  I've been waiting since Sunday for Wilson to have somewhere to put down what he was carrying.  You gave him somewhere to put it.

The captain spoke Portuguese but Sam understood him.  The mechanism is not a translator's mechanism.  It's forgiveness's mechanism.  Forgiveness does not, I have decided today, need a shared language.

The date on that note is 2016.  It's 1994.  Ashford Cade has not been alive yet in any sense we know how to describe.  I don't know what to do with the fact but I do know that we saw it.  Sam.  We saw it.

The note is not a forgery.  It's real handwriting on real paper.  The paper is not from 1994.  I checked the fiber.  It's · a paper we haven't seen yet.  I don't have a mechanism to offer.  I have only a fact.

One of the crew made it up the beach.  Not far.  Just to here.  My grandmother would have known his name from the letter · she may still know it, if we bring her the letter.  I would like to bring my grandmother the letter.

The grave has been here since 1874.  I have kept the moss from covering the letters twice.  My great-great-grandfather is the reason it has a cross.  He came back for it a year later.  He did not come back alone.
My dad's tide-chart binder has papers older than that note.  This note's paper is · newer.  Not from now.  From later.  I do not have · a system for later paper.
I read about it.  Tillamook microfilm.  Three articles between July 25 and April 12 the next spring.  The last article uses the word 'fall.'  The first article doesn't.
My mom was at Sweetgum the year before.  1975.  She said the camp was different in '76 · you can hear it in old campers' voices.  Something · closed off.

It's not something the current camp advertises.  It's not something we hide either.  If a camper asks · like you're asking · I answer.  Andersson's son was thirteen.  His name was Andrew.  I have his sister's phone number if you ever want it.
My grandmother would say · a place remembers the year that broke it.  She would say Camp Sweetgum has a 1976 the way Coimbra has 1755.

The date · July 22 · that's a bad-weather week most years on this coast.  Fog off the bluff.  If Andrew went out alone in fog, the fall theory · is actually possible.  I don't know if I want it to be.

I was fourteen in July of 1976.  I lived in Astoria.  I remember the story on the radio for a week.  I did not know, in 1976, that I would work at that camp one day.  Not everything is a thread I was already pulling.
His name is Peter Halstrom.  He lives on Court Street in Salem.  Two of the three articles quote him.  The third does not.  His silence is why nobody talks about this decade.

Peter has, in my sixteen years of coming to this camp, refused every invitation to visit.  I understand.  I don't understand.  Both.  He was Andrew's counselor.  He was watching that afternoon and then he wasn't.  He gets to carry that however he wants.

Rosemary was twelve.  She saw the search dog come off the trail.  That's the part she talks about.  Not the rest.  Rosemary is a nurse now, in Portland.  She's good at her job.  She sees things people don't want seen and she doesn't flinch.  I think about why sometimes.
Rosemary is Jenny's older sister.  Rosemary is not the reason Jenny came back to this camp.  Jenny came back for other reasons.  I've asked.  Jenny won't say all of them.

Rosemary saw Andrew's father at the search on July 24.  She's mentioned it once.  She said · Andersson looked at Peter with a face she still doesn't have a word for.  Rosemary is 30 now.  She still doesn't have the word.

A.J.  Andrew Andersson-Junior.  He carved it two summers before he vanished.  I was down there in '88 for my own reasons and I saw it.  I did not deface it.  I put a small stone in the alcove next to it.  It is still there.

Andrew signed his name where he expected to come back and read it.  Twelve-year-olds do that with everything.  I did that with my name in a library book I never returned.  I'm going to return it in September.

That clipping was on the shelf when I inherited the boathouse this spring.  It has been on the shelf since 1976.  Sixteen counselors have worked in this boathouse since.  No one has thrown it away.  I understand.  I did not throw it away either.

My dad has mentioned that island twice this year.  Once at a Thanksgiving thing.  Once at a Rotary lunch.  Both times he said · ask me in ten years.  He is not a person who says that lightly.  I have not asked in ten years.  I am asking, though.  I'll remember to ask.
That's the Delisle place.  Bought in '84 by a man who does not need to have bought it.  I don't ask.  Not my acre.
The island is on old topos but the newer nautical charts don't label it.  My dad's Northwest Regional maps stopped naming it around 1985.  Somebody bought the naming.
Rich weirdos buy islands.  Fine.  What's not fine is the government pretending it doesn't see them.  That's the weird part.  Not the island · the pretending.

That's not Coast Guard.  Coast Guard is white with the orange stripe.  This is haze-gray.  Twin hulls · sixty-two feet.  My best guess · a US Fish and Wildlife enforcement platform.  Or DEA.  DEA runs some coast-boats you don't hear about.
I've watched that boat since May.  Every ninety minutes.  I don't ask.  I do keep a log.  I'll show my log to the right person the day the right person exists.

The boat is not our concern.  It has never come inside our marker buoys.  If it ever does · I have the county sheriff's night line taped to my flashlight.  Some counselors carry an emergency granola bar.  I carry a phone number.

That's a Boeing 44-foot Response Boat · Medium hull configured for enforcement.  The seams gave it away.  My dad's pharmacy is in Newport · we see them in port on Thursdays.  Ford is right about the hull count.  I know what to look for because I look for allergen labels for a living.

That guy's not fishing.  He's watching.  He asked me on Tuesday if we had any campers with parents 'in law enforcement or federal service.'  I said no.  He nodded like I had said something specific.  Which I had, I guess.  I don't know what he was asking.

The Rockport polo has a specific weave that clings differently than fishing gear.  He hasn't cast the line since Sunday.  I've watched him not cast.  It is · unhurried.  He's on someone's clock but the clock isn't racing.
He didn't come in on the campground road.  He came in on the water.  Kayak.  Left it at the boathouse cove.  I noticed on Sunday.  I did not tell anyone.  I'm telling you.

'Ask me about the small island north of Nehalem in ten years.'  My dad's phrase for something-I-can't-tell-you-until-a-thing-that-will-happen-happens.  He said it about the salt-marsh project too.  Salt-marsh cleared up in eight.  Two more years.
The drift to 1600.3 · that's not a receiver problem.  That's an intentional signal offset.  Whoever runs Station 1600 wants to be found by people who tune carefully.  They found us.
Volta.  They said the word before Wilson did.  They knew.  Somebody who runs a pirate radio out of · wherever · knows what happens Friday.  I'm not scared.  I feel · seen.

'The twelve-year-old at the closing-bonfire seat, who is walking tonight.'  They mean me.  They mean me specifically.  Sam.  I am · fine with this actually.  I've been feeling watched all summer.  It's nice to know by whom.

'The fisherman is not the reason.  The reason is on the island.'  Confirmation.  My dad's 'ask me in ten years' is what's on the island.  The fisherman is checking on it.  The pirate radio is · commenting on it.  Three parties are watching, and they all know each other.

The Ana Faustina.  Yes.  I've heard this transmission twice before.  1988 and 1993.  Station 1600 has been broadcasting my line's history at me for six years.  I've never spoken to them.  I never will.  We are, evidently, on the same task.

Every counselor I've ever asked knows about Station 1600.  Nobody knows who runs it.  Nobody has tried very hard to find out.  My theory · the station is somebody who used to counsel here.  My other theory · the station is Peter Halstrom.  My third theory · both my first two theories are wrong and it's much older than either of them.
I've read three of the books.  They're not good.  I've read them twice each anyway.  There's a specific badness that a specific reader loves.  I am that reader.  Don't tell my mom.
Sam!  Did you see Jonnie's fur cloak?  He's a NINETEEN-YEAR-OLD in a FUR CLOAK on a MOUNTAIN.  This game does not want you to take it seriously and it will not let you.

Ines Rocha wrote Fey Faire · the dialogue lead credit is hers.  My cousin's studio partner knows her.  Rocha said in an interview that Fey Faire is the game she'd remake if she could remake anything.  She'd remake it slower.  She'd give it more silence.

I check that cart out carefully.  Not because it's dark · because it's honest.  The older-sister-who-hasn't-aged plot · I lose two campers a summer to that ending.  Not to the game.  To the ending's shape.  You'll see.

You've played all three.  I've played all three.  Not this summer · earlier in my life.  Northwind Harbor in '89 in Astoria.  The Hubbard cart in '86 in a friend's basement.  Fey Faire in '91 on a rainy weekend I do not talk about.  You're going to remember these three, Sam.  I did.

Rocha was seventeen when Andrew vanished.  My cousin's studio partner told me she used to visit Camp Sweetgum for weekend trips with a cousin here.  She wasn't at camp that July.  She was in Portland.  She heard about it the way everyone did.  She wrote Fey Faire fourteen years later.  I don't think she was ready before that.

The older-sister-who-hasn't-aged-since-1976 is a cipher for what a specific loss does to a specific summer.  Rocha didn't have to explain that in the game.  She trusted the players who'd read the microfilm.  There aren't many of us.  There are enough of us.

Station 1600 has been reading the summers before they finish since 1988 at the earliest.  Someone at that station has a subscription to the shape of what's about to happen.  I have my theories.  I don't say them out loud.  I don't want to be wrong about who they are.

So somebody knows me.  Not casually.  Specifically.  I have never been · named before.  I don't hate it.  It's the first time somebody has bothered to know something small enough about me to be true.  I don't know who they are.  I am not going to try to find out.  I want them to keep watching.

So Chapter 2 wasn't 'damaged.'  Chapter 2 was withheld.  The 220-Hz tone is masking a chapter that was written and mastered and then pulled.  Somebody at Oneironautics realized what they'd drawn a map of · four miles off Nehalem, one cabin, a dock · and blacked it out.  This is a much bigger studio than I thought.

There's a specific pattern of solder on my Northwind Harbor cart that isn't on standard Rev-2 carts of that year.  I noticed it in April.  I thought it was a QC variation.  It's not.  It's a REDACTION.  Oneironautics · or someone at Oneironautics · physically modified the ROM after the run.  Sam.  I have your cart.  I want to look at it under a microscope.  I know a place.

Three parties, one island.  Fed boat, pirate radio, and · whoever writes the notes on the hunter's-cabin door with dates from the future.  I keep my log.  I don't share it.  Some day I'll share it.  I'm waiting for the day it becomes obvious who to share it with.  It has not become obvious.
I've had a feeling for eight years.  I have not put words on the feeling.  You have put some words on it, Sam.  Thank you.  It is · a little easier to carry with words on it.  Not much.  A little.

It's the same bar of melody.  My grandmother taught me both.  The music box is the folk tune.  The shanty is the folk tune slowed and given English weight.  Rocha's grandmother taught her the same tune.  Coimbra is small.  My grandmother might have taught Rocha's grandmother.  Or the reverse.  Or a third woman we neither of us knew.  My grandmother says · a song is not owned.  A song is loaned.

Peter has been in Salem since 1977.  He works for the state.  I have not asked which department.  Bear will not say what he heard.  I understand · I would not want to be quoted on someone else's guess.  There is a possibility that Peter is watching the island from a desk sixty miles inland.  There is a possibility that Peter has been watching this camp longer than I've been the head counselor.  I don't know.  I might not want to know.  I am · not fully sure I want to know.

The specific phrasing the fisherman used with Bear · 'parents in law enforcement or federal service' · is a form phrase.  Not a spoken phrase.  It is · the language of a document.  Peter Halstrom went into work that involves forms with that exact phrase.  I know because my dad has friends in that field.  I don't want to be right about this.  I might be.
